sea","400 metres","mean depth 41.8 meters","112 registered lakes"],"similarityValue":0,"id":5,"type":"tools","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Call tools to help you with the reasoning process."},{"role":"system","content":"Tool wikipedia_query_run with arguments {\"query\":\"Maximum depth of the Black Sea\"} gave result: Page: List of lakes by depth\nSummary: These articles lists the world's deepest lakes.\n\n\n== Lakes ranked by maximum depth ==\nThis list contains all lakes whose maximum depth is reliably known to exceed 400 metres (1,300 ft)\nGeologically, the Caspian Sea, like the Black and Mediterranean seas, is a remnant of the ancient Tethys Ocean. The deepest area is oceanic rather than continental crust. However, it is generally regarded by geographers as a large endorheic salt lake. Of these lakes, 10 have a deepest point above sea level. These are Issyk-Kul, Crater Lake, Quesnel Lake, Sarez Lake, Lake Toba, Lake Tahoe, Lake Kivu, Nahuel Huapi Lake, Lake Van, and Lake Poso.\n\n\n== Lakes ranked by mean depth ==\nMean depth can be a more useful indicator than maximum depth for many ecological purposes. Unfortunately, accurate mean depth figures are only available for well-studied lakes, as they must be calculated by dividing the lake's volume by its surface area. A reliable volume figure requires a bathymetric survey. Therefore, mean depth figures are not available for many deep lakes in remote locations. The average lake on Earth has the mean depth 41.8 meters (137.14 feet)\nThe Caspian Sea ranks much further down the list on mean depth, as it has a large continental shelf (significantly larger than the oceanic basin that contains its greatest depths).\nOf the 112 registered lakes; 58 are known to be cryptodepressions. These include: Vostok               (subglacial surface), (Caspian Sea) (subsea surface), Dead Sea (subsea surface) and Jökulsárlón (glacial lagoon estuary). The remaining: 54 lakes got their whole basins above the sea level.\nThis list contains all lakes whose mean depth is reliably known to exceed 100 metres (328 ft).\n\n\n== Greatest maximum depth by continent ==\nAfrica — 1: Tanganyika, 2: Malawi, 3: Kivu\nAntarctica — 1: Radok (surface lake); Vostok (subglacial lake)\nAsia — 1: Baikal, 2: Issyk Kul, 3: Matano\nEurasia — 1: Baikal, 2: Caspian Sea, 3: Issyk Kul\nEurope — 1: H."}],"parents":[3],"children":[7],"textembedding":[0.005514354,0.027258502,-0.037798,-0.045213412,-0.07140767,-0.052045204,-0.0010148986,-0.0134919295,-0.09852885,0.13066229,0.061795097,-0.044183493,-0.050019696,-0.04133405,-0.005647385,0.030691564,0.0074197026,-0.03252825,-0.030863216,0.00501656,-0.026915196,0.004870655,0.04294759,0.09743027,0.037489023,0.032579746,0.11013259,0.011783982,-0.018212387,-0.054963306,0.16616015,0.004711876,-0.021010332,0.0024546385,0.020598365,-0.12537538,0.0075999387,0.023670955,0.058293372,0.0068489565,0.018813174,0.053899053,0.06745964,0.08665045,-0.026434569,-0.08582652,-0.082599446,-0.032802895,0.06776862,-0.12228563,0.007857418,-0.09145674,-0.058362033,0.0718883,-0.069656804,-0.012316106,0.09248666,0.16574818,0.04799419,-0.012719491,0.051152606,0.008007615,-0.089946195,-0.00970698,0.08589518,-0.06553713,0.025490478,-0.0021338619,-0.10450237,0.08747439,0.10134396,0.054963306,0.05201087,0.07442876,0.050465994,0.10024538,0.05698881,-0.09317327,0.058430694,0.06776862,0.05915164,0.09674366,-0.011801147,-0.053349767,0.069176175,0.0076685995,-0.16808265,-0.12462011,-0.07209428,-0.017748924,0.03233943,0.13251615,0.07916638,0.062790684,0.0440805,0.017851917,-0.025988271,0.014169958,-0.008874462,0.031635657,0.017886247,0.051186938,0.038724925,-0.033146203,0.020512538,-0.06941649,-0.009071863,0.050465994,0.007518403,0.08122622,-0.012178783,0.0065657287,-0.081020236,-0.043771524,-0.037077058,-0.12242295,0.12077508,-0.12750389,0.013706495,0.029095192,-0.009372256,-0.05640519,0.032184947,0.03618446,-0.10086333,0.12077508,-0.05005403,-0.01083989,0.03594415,0.057881407,0.056370858,0.03958319,-0.026589056,0.030605737,0.039823506,0.021113325,0.020735687,0.021061828,-0.035326198,0.035017222,-0.16423763,-0.07628261,-0.0029245387,0.036493436,-0.04682695,0.008033362,-0.052731816,0.008471077,-0.1003827,-0.09894081,0.068455234,-0.029524323,0.07312419,0.031807307,-0.009337925,0.02145663,-0.061417457,0.002699244,-0.024409063,0.044149164,-0.10292316,-0.10 zone","nearshore","intertidal zone","photosynthesis"],"similarityValue":0,"id":6,"type":"tools","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Call tools to help you with the reasoning process."},{"role":"system","content":"Tool wikipedia_query_run with arguments {\"query\":\"sunlight penetration in seawater\"} gave result: Page: Littoral zone\nSummary: The littoral zone, also called litoral or nearshore, is the part of a sea, lake, or river that is close to the shore. In coastal ecology, the littoral zone includes the intertidal zone extending from the high water mark (which is rarely inundated), to coastal areas that are permanently submerged — known as the foreshore — and the terms are often used interchangeably. However, the geographical meaning of littoral zone extends well beyond the intertidal zone to include all neritic waters within the bounds of continental shelves.\n\n\n== Etymology ==\nThe word littoral may be used both as a noun and as an adjective. It derives from the Latin noun litus, litoris, meaning \"shore\". (The doubled t is a late-medieval innovation, and the word is sometimes seen in the more classical-looking spelling litoral.)\n\n\n== Description ==\nThe term has no single definition. What is regarded as the full extent of the littoral zone, and the way the littoral zone is divided into subregions, varies in different contexts. For lakes, the littoral zone is the nearshore habitat where photosynthetically active radiation penetrates to the lake bottom in sufficient quantities to support photosynthesis. The use of the term also varies from one part of the world to another, and between different disciplines. For example, military commanders speak of the littoral in ways that are quite different from the definition used by marine biologists.\nThe adjacency of water gives a number of distinctive characteristics to littoral regions. The erosive power of water results in particular types of landforms, such as sand dunes, and estuaries. The natural movement of the littoral along the coast is called the littoral drift. Biologically, the ready availability of water enables a greater variety of plant and animal life, and particularly the formation of extensive wetlands. In addition, the additional local humidity due to evaporation usually creates a microclimate supporting unique type."}],"parents":[4],"children":[9],"textembedding":[-0.028016934,0.0256109,-0.01756405,-0.024987113,-0.052825823,-0.11142613,0.070862174,-0.03774801,-0.12782282,-0.05093664,0.028444674,-0.0040880307,-0.060239974,-0.043486845,-0.033345856,0.016441235,-0.07856148,-0.0614519,0.023828652,0.08961142,0.02676936,0.06826009,-0.07635149,0.10472488,0.0044890367,0.015024348,0.032258686,-0.003994463,-0.0000017143692,-0.06626397,0.17009772,-0.011816302,0.04049267,0.061986577,-0.01439165,-0.14022726,0.036643013,0.015443177,0.0342548,0.07007798,0.031617075,0.061630126,-0.0063403463,0.037284624,-0.02999523,-0.035716247,-0.05667548,-0.039744124,0.0035110281,-0.09638396,-0.04256008,-0.020442382,-0.053681303,0.008483499,0.012350977,0.017590785,0.1546991,0.12589799,-0.089754,0.042132337,0.0031568063,-0.078775354,-0.021190926,-0.018517554,0.06376883,0.010381593,-0.0070799794,0.03630439,-0.04929697,0.029300153,0.029585313,0.020032465,-0.045304738,0.08889852,-0.0019760672,0.08790046,0.093817525,-0.010016233,-0.028444674,0.006790364,0.007908724,0.091037214,-0.023596961,-0.0014213426,0.047586016,-0.0072537484,-0.07036314,-0.12675346,0.045411672,0.04854843,0.040314443,0.017929412,0.04840585,0.054928876,0.06640655,-0.041169923,-0.006892843,0.009802363,0.051613893,-0.0040056016,0.019497791,-0.020513672,-0.06480253,0.06751154,0.091393664,-0.0033082974,-0.008140417,0.10479617,0.028088225,0.13723308,-0.030458614,-0.10222973,-0.027250567,-0.027161457,-0.09738202,-0.05221986,0.080486305,-0.10422585,0.07414151,0.19291049,0.005230897,-0.06861654,0.058172565,0.042346206,0.03375577,-0.02618122,0.024969291,0.0628777,0.069828466,0.13131602,-0.04423539,0.0034664718,-0.0036825694,0.06013304,-0.0702562,0.056140803,0.042025402,0.0356806,-0.0041125366,0.03913816,0.06854525,-0.04698005,-0.07891793,0.018250218,-0.008456766,-0.07834761,-0.08996787,0.003535534,-0.028462498,-0.105295196,0.03856784,-0.012119285,0.13680534,-0.016280834,0.058172565,0.0041949656,-0.03215175,0.03831833,0.056532897,0.029656602,-0.031 Eventually, various nations adopted the name <strong>Black</strong> <strong>Sea</strong>, usually translated into their own language (for example, Karadeniz in Turkish). The name may have been derived from the color of the water at great <strong>depth</strong> or from <strong>the</strong> <strong>sea</strong>&#x27;s ...\"},{\"title\":\"Black Sea | Infoplease\",\"link\":\"https://www.infoplease.com/encyclopedia/places/other/oceans-continents/black-sea\",\"snippet\":\"<strong>Black</strong> <strong>Sea</strong>, inland <strong>sea</strong>, c.159,600 sq mi (413,360 sq km), between SE Europe and Asia, connected with the Mediterranean <strong>Sea</strong> by the Bosporus, <strong>the</strong> <strong>Sea</strong> <strong>of</strong> Marmara, and the Dardanelles. The littoral zone of <strong>the</strong> <strong>Black</strong> <strong>Sea</strong> is often referred to as the Pontic littoral or Pontic zone.\"},{\"title\":\"Commission on the Protection of the Black Sea Against Pollution\",\"link\":\"http://www.blacksea-commission.org/_geography.asp\",\"snippet\":\"The shelf occupies a large area in the north-western part of <strong>the</strong> <strong>Black</strong> <strong>Sea</strong>, where it is over 200 km wide and has a <strong>depth</strong> ranging from 0 to 160 meters. eternal darkness below <strong>1,000 meters (3,280 feet),</strong> an area known as the aphotic zone, which includes the midnight zone (or bathypelagic zone) between 1,000 and 4,000 meters (3,280 and 13,123 feet), the abyss (or abyssopelagic zone) between 4,000 and 6,000 meters ...\"},{\"title\":\"What is the deepest any amount of sunlight can reach in the ocean? - far <strong>sunlight</strong> <strong>can</strong> travel below the surface. At this level everything lives in the dark. Some creatures down there have adapted to life in these low level light and some even produce there own headlamps. These creatures n...\"},{\"title\":\"Light in the Ocean | manoa.hawaii.edu/ExploringOurFluidEarth\",\"link\":\"https://manoa.hawaii.edu/exploringourfluidearth/physical/ocean-depths/light-ocean\",\"snippet\":\"At <strong>40 m</strong>, saltwater has absorbed nearly all the red visible light, yet blue light is still able to penetrate beyond these depths. At this depth, a scuba diver without a flashlight sees all underwater features only in shades of blue (Fig. 9.8 A). To see a full spectrum of colors, a diver must ...\"},{\"title\":\"Sunlight can penetrate into the ocean only up to a depth of ________.\",\"link\":\"https://www.toppr.com/ask/en-gb/question/sunlight-can-penetrate-into-the-ocean-only-up-to-a-depth-of/\",\"snippet\":\"Click here:point_up_2:to get an answer to your question :writing_hand:<strong>sunlight</strong> <strong>can</strong> <strong>penetrate</strong> into the ocean only up to a <strong>depth</strong> of\"},{\"title\":\"Light Transmission in the Ocean - river, sea, depth, oceans, percentage, important, plants, source, marine\",\"link\":\"http://www.waterencyclopedia.com/La-Mi/Light-Transmission-in-the-Ocean.html\",\"snippet\":\"Once it is within the water, light may be scattered or absorbed by solid particles. Most of the visible light spectrum is absorbed within 10 meters (33 feet) of the water&#x27;s surface, and almost none penetrates below <strong>150 meters (490 feet)</strong> of water depth, even when the water is very clear.\"},{\"title\":\"optics - Why can't sunlight reach the very deep parts of an ocean? - Physics Stack Exchange\",\"link\":\"https://physics.stackexchange.com/questions/773369/why-cant-sunlight-reach-the-very-deep-parts-of-an-ocean\",\"snippet\":\"The euphotic zone extends to about<strong>200 m</strong>. That is the maximum depth at which photosynthesis can occur - so some light can penetrate to that depth. The &#x27;rule-of-thumb&#x27; of 50% at 50 m, is for blue wavelengths around 500 nm - red light fades much faster (as shown by the graph).\"},{\"title\":\"National Ocean Service - How far does light travel in the ocean? Sunlight entering the water may travel about 1,000 meters (3,280 feet) into the ocean under the right conditions, but there is rarely any significant light beyond 200 meters (656 feet). The ocean is divided into three zones based on depth and light level. The upper 200 meters (656 feet) of the ocean is called the euphotic, or \\\"sunlight,\\\" zone. This zone contains the vast majority of commercial fisheries and is home to many protected marine mam\",\"link\":\"https://m.facebook.com/usoceangov/photos/how-far-does-light-travel-in-the-oceansunlight-entering-the-water-may-travel-abo/10157466355137867/\",\"snippet\":\"How far does light travel in the ocean? <strong>Sunlight</strong> entering the water may travel about 1,000 meters (3,280 feet) into the ocean under the right...\"},{\"title\":\"Dispatches from the Deep: Light and Dark in the Sea | AMNH\",\"link\":\"https://www.amnh.org/learn-teach/curriculum-collections/deep-sea-vents/light-and-dark-in-the-sea\",\"snippet\":\"The greatest ocean depth ever measured, at the Mariana Trench in the Western Pacific, is about <strong>11,000 meters</strong>, almost seven miles!) Below the photic zone, from 200 to 1,000 meters, is the aphotic (a meaning without and photic meaning light) zone. In the aphotic zone; all that’s left of sunlight ...\"},{\"title\":\"How far does light travel in the ocean?\",\"link\":\"https://oceanservice.noaa.gov/facts/light_travel.html\",\"snippet\":\"Such a minuscule amount of light penetrates beyond a depth of <strong>200 meters</strong> that photosynthesis is no longer possible. The aphotic zone exists in depths below 1,000 meters. Sunlight does not penetrate to these depths and the zone is bathed in darkness. The aphotic zone is further subdivided into ...\"},{\"title\":\"How Deep Light Penetrates the Ocean - American Oceans\",\"link\":\"https://www.americanoceans.org/facts/how-deep-does-light-penetrate-the-ocean/\",\"snippet\":\"The deep sea is pitch black, so many people wonder how far down light actually <strong>penetrates</strong> since it doesn&#x27;t reach the bottom. Learn the answer in this guide.\"},{\"title\":\"Photic zone - Wikipedia\",\"link\":\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photic_zone\",\"snippet\":\"The ocean can be divided into depth layers depending on the amount of light penetration, as discussed in pelagic zone. The upper <strong>200 metres</strong> is referred to as the photic or euphotic zone. This represents the region where enough light can penetrate to support photosynthesis, and it corresponds ...\"},{\"title\":\"Estimation of the depth of sunlight penetration in the sea for remote sensing - PubMed\",\"link\":\"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20134900/\",\"snippet\":\"Penetration depths as a function of wavelength are presented for a variety of water types, and a mean penetration depth z (90) for a broadband sensor is defined and applied to the MSS on ERTS-1. The maximum z (90) expected for ERTS-l is found to be somewhat <strong>less than 20 m</strong>.\"},{\"title\":\"Light penetration in water - Responsible Seafood Advocate\",\"link\":\"https://www.globalseafood.org/advocate/light-penetration-in-water/\",\"snippet\":\"Light <strong>penetrating</strong> water is scattered and absorbed exponentially as it passes downward. The presence of dissolved organic matter and suspended solids further impedes light <strong>penetration</strong>, and different types of solids absorb different wavelengths.\"},{\"title\":\"At what depth does sunlight not enter into the sea? - Quora\",\"link\":\"https://www.quora.com/At-what-depth-does-sunlight-not-enter-into-the-sea\",\"snippet\":\"Answer (1 of 9): The <strong>depth</strong> of the ocean is classified into following zones [1] based on the reach of the <strong>sunlight</strong> below sea-level. # Upto 200metres below the surface of the ocean is called the euphotic, or &quot;<strong>sunlight</strong>,&quot; zone. Scattering occurs when light is bounced between air and water molecules, dust and other objects. Absorption occurs when light’s electromagnetic energy is converted to heat in the molecules of <strong>seawater</strong>. There are many factors that influence light <strong>penetration</strong>...\"},{\"title\":\"How Far Can Light Penetrate Underwater? clear answer to the question. If you haven't found a solution yet, try to summarize the most important findings so far. Make sure that your final answer is founded on the reasoning steps you have taken so far."},{"role":"assistant","content":"The maximum depth of the Black Sea is approximately 2,212 meters (7,257 feet).\n\nTo determine if sunlight can reach this depth, we need to understand the penetration ability of sunlight in seawater. Sunlight generally penetrates seawater to a maximum depth of about 200 meters (656 feet) in clear water, and even less in murky water.\n\nGiven that the Black Sea's maximum depth is 2,212 meters, it is far deeper than the maximum penetration depth of sunlight. Therefore, sunlight cannot reach the deepest part of the Black Sea.\n\n**Conclusion:** Sunlight does not travel to the deepest part of the Black Sea, which is approximately 2,212 meters deep. Sunlight penetration in seawater is limited to about 200 meters under the best conditions."}],"parents":[12],"children":[],"textembedding":[-0.042503882,0.03526107,-0.0055103926,-0.043347966,0.065294236,-0.11174627,0.13352917,-0.016323559,-0.050073437,0.005700993,0.02598972,0.07188357,0.044164825,0.010469406,0.006997756,0.10935015,0.13363808,0.002741582,0.0032640311,0.014349484,0.015166343,-0.010802956,-0.10123602,0.11686525,-0.08005215,0.027078865,0.0383379,0.01850185,-0.040053304,-0.03104063,0.13342026,0.015928745,0.08876531,0.014213341,0.0062183365,-0.055219647,-0.017508004,-0.13363808,0.051489327,-0.047377802,0.017126804,-0.018774135,-0.009230503,0.010952714,-0.0447094,-0.027814038,-0.055083506,-0.03324615,-0.010020133,-0.092686236,-0.0227359,-0.08936434,-0.12340012,0.07722037,-0.022749515,0.03858296,0.16620351,0.1071174,-0.0137640685,0.041496422,0.17785737,-0.042830624,-0.03934536,0.0031380989,-0.054838445,-0.06910624,0.079888776,-0.033055548,-0.072863795,-0.0013273953,-0.013757261,0.075586654,0.038719103,0.03918199,0.09900327,0.067309156,-0.017712219,-0.032347605,0.047377802,0.01979521,0.03618684,0.011640237,-0.081631415,-0.0838097,0.101889506,-0.0010083099,-0.1886399,-0.015452243,0.034063008,-0.033110004,0.084735475,0.12448926,-0.019495694,0.024696361,-0.04666986,-0.0015945763,-0.012361795,0.008672317,0.019427622,0.08065118,0.019604608,-0.03398132,0.07945312,0.04882092,-0.093394175,-0.07449751,-0.0018498446,0.022381928,0.043647483,0.02285843,-0.050400183,0.015438629,-0.023498302,0.0012797453,-0.034716494,-0.07738375,0.048303578,0.048303578,0.107444145,0.053068586,-0.03918199,-0.07749266,-0.06186343,0.040870164,0.042013764,0.002790934,-0.029052941,-0.13113305,0.06600218,-0.09611704,-0.029706428,-0.09644378,0.020693753,0.0055682533,0.08212153,0.035887323,-0.08696822,0.086750396,-0.03836513,-0.06703687,-0.06153669,-0.044736627,-0.020666525,0.073408365,0.112290844,0.03259266,-0.14997526,0.0044961264,-0.12078617,-0.06997756,0.009278153,-0.039399818,0.060175255,-0.04522674,0.067309156,-0.09927556,0.0013052721,0.019836051,-0.052251726,0.088983
